title: Storage IOPS Guide
summary: Understand transaction demand, read/write operations, RAID write penalty, and headroom before storage performance becomes the compute bottleneck.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the random input/output operations per second required by a workload after read activity, write activity, RAID penalty, and reserve are considered.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when sizing storage for databases, virtual machines, logging, application workloads, analytics systems, or any random-I/O-heavy compute environment.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible Storage IOPS Estimator inputs and explain how each value affects random storage pressure.

items.label: Transactions per Second
items.description: The number of workload transactions, requests, events, or operations expected each second.
items.whyItMatters: Transactions are the activity driver that creates read and write operations.
items.commonMistake: Estimating storage only by capacity while ignoring how frequently the workload touches disk.

items.label: Reads per Transaction
items.description: The average number of storage read operations created by each transaction.
items.whyItMatters: Read operations contribute to total IOPS and can still create latency even if cache helps some requests.
items.commonMistake: Assuming reads do not matter because the system may cache part of the workload.

items.label: Writes per Transaction
items.description: The average number of storage write operations created by each transaction.
items.whyItMatters: Writes are often more expensive than reads, especially with redundancy or parity.
items.commonMistake: Underestimating writes because the capacity written per transaction appears small.

items.label: RAID Write Penalty
items.description: The multiplier applied to write operations because of the selected redundancy method or storage behavior.
items.whyItMatters: Write penalty can make a write-heavy workload demand far more backend IOPS than the raw transaction count suggests.
items.commonMistake: Using front-end write count as if it equals backend disk work.

items.label: Headroom (%)
items.description: Extra IOPS reserve above the calculated workload requirement.
items.whyItMatters: Headroom protects the design during bursts, maintenance, compaction, rebuilds, and measurement error.
items.commonMistake: Running storage at its advertised maximum with no practical reserve.

links.label: Check throughput counterpart
links.note: High IOPS can also create high MB/s demand depending on I/O size.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the IOPS plan has reasonable margin. Watch means workload assumptions deserve review. Risk means storage can become the first practical bottleneck even when CPU and RAM look acceptable.
bullets: Estimated Required IOPS includes read load, write penalty, and headroom.
Write penalty stress helps show whether writes are dominating the design.
IOPS pressure and throughput pressure should both be checked before choosing storage.

sections.title: How this fits in the Compute flow
body: Storage IOPS follows RAM Sizing because memory pressure can increase disk pressure through paging, cache misses, and background activity.
bullets: Use RAM results as context, not as a replacement for storage math.
Continue into Storage Throughput when random IOPS demand appears reasonable.
A storage design can pass IOPS and still fail on sequential throughput or transfer-window needs.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most compute planning problems come from trusting one comfortable number while ignoring the rest of the platform. CPU, RAM, storage, network, power, cooling, protection timing, and growth all interact.
bullets: Sizing only for average load instead of peak behavior.
Ignoring headroom, background tasks, maintenance windows, and growth.
Treating theoretical maximums as sustained real-world capacity.
Forgetting that one weak resource can make the rest of the system feel undersized.
Failing to document assumptions before hardware, licensing, or architecture decisions are locked in.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, application requirements, benchmark testing, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Vendor specifications still matter.
Application behavior may change the final requirement.
Site conditions, operating procedures, and maintenance expectations may affect the design.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, application testing, compliance review, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, platform, and software being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
Use the results as decision support, not as a final authority.
